quote: Wonder is the beginning of wisdom.
through_line: The metrics are changing. The models of mind are changing. What holds steady across today's pieces — in search analytics, in children's curiosity research, in the Buddhist concept of self-overcoming — is the irreducibility of genuine surprise. You can't optimise your way to wonder.

articles.title: Modern SEO KPIs for DMOs & CVBs
articles.pitch: As AI-driven search reshapes how destination content is discovered, the old KPIs — rankings, traffic, impressions — are increasingly poor proxies for actual visibility and authority. This piece proposes a new measurement framework built for the AI era, with concrete metrics DMOs can start tracking today.

articles.title: Question-asking practice fosters aspects of curiosity in science content in young children
articles.pitch: A 2025 study found that practising question-asking — not just answering questions — measurably increased children's intrinsic motivation to learn new science content. The mechanism is direct: asking questions changes how much children value new information. Practical implications for anyone designing activities that aim to cultivate curiosity rather than just deliver content.

articles.title: AI as a Buddhist self-overcoming technique in another medium
articles.pitch: An open-access paper argues that Buddhist philosophy — which aims at overcoming fixed self-concepts — finds an unexpected ally in AI, a system unburdened by the organic substrate that generates attachment in the first place. The provocation: the very features that make AI not-quite-conscious may make it uniquely suited to model something the Buddhist path has always sought.

articles.title: The most beautiful science of the year
articles.pitch: Nautilus editors selected the most striking scientific insights of 2025 — not the most viral, not the most cited, but the ones that opened something up. A piece to read slowly, without an agenda, as a reminder that science at its best is not a production system but a form of attention.
